1999-05-01.title: Bombs Pound Heart and Homes of Serbia's Capital
1999-05-01.abstract: NATO bombs residential areas of Belgrade, Serbia, for first time, with two missiles meant for military office buildings hitting houses; worst night of bombing, coupled with earthquake, shakes city's residents; they comment, interviews; at least four people die and 37 are hurt; map; photo (M)

1999-05-01.title: NATO Says New Bombing Is the Strongest Effort Yet
1999-05-01.abstract: NATO unleashes what officials call most aggressive bombing in Kosovo conflict, hitting Serbian tanks and armored vehicles in Kosovo and bridges in Serbia; takes advantage of clear weather to schedule 650 missions in day; State Department says it will fly first of 20,000 Kosovar refugees to United States next week; Sec Madeleine K Albright says Clinton Adminstration changes rules to let first 400 Kosovar Albanians go directly to Fort Dix, NJ, for processing and immediate reunion with families around US; Republican leaders in Senate move to block vote on bipartisan measure championed by Sen John McCain to instruct Pres Clinton to use all necessary force and other means against Serbia (M)

1999-05-01.title: 3 CAPTIVE SOLDIERS TELL OF ISOLATION IN YUGOSLAV CELLS
1999-05-01.abstract: Three American soldiers held in Yugoslavia, allowed to meet with Rev Jesse L Jackson, Rep Rod R Blagojevich and New York Times reporter in presence of military court chairman, describe their days as lonely and monotonous; seem fit; say they are well fed, have no medical problems and have no contact with one another or other prisoners; Specialist Steven M Gonzales, Sgt Andrew A Ramirez and Sgt Christopher J Stone, taped by CNN, send greetings to families; photo (M)

1999-05-01.title: After Seeing Milosevic, Russian Hints at Progress
1999-05-01.abstract: Russian envoy Viktor S Chernomyrdin says he made some progress toward Kosovo peace settlement in talks, Belgrade, with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; apparently refers to Milosevic's willingness to let lightly armed United Nations observers monitor Kosovo; NATO officials are dismissive; photo (M)

1999-05-01.title: An Albanian Tells How Serbs Chose Her, 'the Most Beautiful,' for Rape
1999-05-01.abstract: Sherife Alickaj, 18, one of small group of ethnic Albanian women willing to say openly that they were raped by Serbian soldiers during chaotic exodus from Kosovo, describes attack by Pristina policemen; she and two friends, in separate interviews, give matching accounts of attack; human rights investigators say relatively few women are reporting sexual assaults so far; note intense social stigma that accompanies rape in Kosovar Albanian society (M)

1999-05-02.title: NATO Says G.I. Releases Won't Alter The Air War
1999-05-02.abstract: Clinton Adm and Pentagon officials say that release of three American prisoners will not alter aims or method's of NATO's air war against Yugoslavia, which continues with unrelenting intensity made possible by clearer skies over Balkans (M)

1999-05-02.title: Serbs Say Allied Missile Killed 34 On a Bus
1999-05-02.abstract: Serbian officials say that 34 or more people are killed when NATO missile strikes bus in village of Luzane, in central Kosovo; photo (M)

1999-05-02.title: New Alliance Highlights Ebb of Russian Clout
1999-05-02.abstract: In shadow of NATO's exgravagant 50th anniversary celebrations last weekend, representatives of five former Soviet republics gather at Washington embassy of Uzbekistan to signal their growing distance from Moscow, another rebuke for world's largest ex-superpower; five leaders--three of them former members of all-powerful Politburo--jointly announce enlargement of regional bloc bound by common economic interests, and an openly pro-NATO tilt; officially, emergence of expanded bloc--which last week added central Asian country of Uzbekistan to group that includes Georgia, Ukraine, Azerbaijan and Moldova--gets cool reception from Moscow (M)

1999-05-02.title: Serbs Release 3 Captured U.S. Soldiers
1999-05-02.abstract: Three American soldiers held captive for more than month are released and handed over to Rev Jesse L Jackson for their trip home; Pres Slobodan Milosevic agrees to free Americans after a lobbying effort by Jackson that was not sanctioned by United States officials; soldiers say they have no ill will toward Yugoslav people and were treated well; soldiers are Staff Sgt Christopher J Stone, Staff Sgt Andrew A Ramirez and Specialist Steven M Gonzales; Jackson, after winning Milosevic's promise to release men, urges NATO to reciprocate by immediately halting further air strikes and opening negotiations on Kosovo; photo of Jackson in prayer with Milosevic, Foreign Min Zivadin Jovanovic and Repr Rod R Blagojevich of Illinois (L)

1999-05-02.title: Yielding, Yet Advancing
1999-05-02.abstract: News analysis of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic's move to release three captured US soldiers and offer to accept lightly armed United Nations peacekeeping force to monitor peace in Kosovo; Milosevic is seen making it clear that he wants a bombing halt, and that he is ready to deal; photo of Milosevic with Rev Jesse L Jackson (M)

1999-05-03.title: Colombia's Offer to Rebels Appears Futile
1999-05-03.abstract: Columbian Govt, which ceded Switzerland-sized area of country nearly six months ago to country's main Marxist guerrilla group in good-faith gesture aimed at jump-starting peace talks, says it may retract concession if talks fail; Pres Andres Pastrana warns Revolutionary Armed Forces of Columbia it will have to withdraw from ceded area if agreement is not reached by May 7 to begin formal talks; rebels appear reluctant to budge, either from jungle stronghold in San Vicenta del Caguan or from sweeping list of demands that have stalled discussions; maps (M)

1999-05-03.title: A WOMAN'S PLACE: A special report; Lower-Caste Women Turn Village Rule Upside Down
1999-05-03.abstract: Women, who are traditionally expected to veil faces and sumbit to male elders in India, are challenging centuries-old feudal hierarchies in epic social experiment being played out in more than 500,000 northern villages, special Report, A Woman's Place; almost million women have been elected to village governing councils since India adopted constitutional amendment in 1993 that sets aside third of all council seats and village chiefs' positions for women, and percentage of them for women from lowest rungs of caste system; even though third of new women council members are just rubber stamps for husbands, two-thirds are engaged in learning ropes and exercising power; photos; map (L)

1999-05-03.title: Man Charged in London Attacks Aimed at Minorities
1999-05-03.abstract: British police charge David Copeland, 22-year-old engineer, with murder for nail-bomb attacks on ethnic minorities and gays in recent days that killed 3 people, wounded more than 100 and confronted Britons with violent challenge to their avowed tolerance; Scotland Yard says Copeland acted alone and was not associated with fringe neo-Nazi groups, Combat 18 and White Wolves, that took responsibility for attacks; police officials say explosive materials were found during raid on Copeland's modest three-bedroom home in Hampshire village of Cove, southwest of London; identify Andrea Dykes and John Light as two of three fatalities in bombing of SoHo pub on April 30; photo (M)

1999-05-03.title: NATO Admits Missile Hit Bus but Says Bridge Was a Legitimate Target
1999-05-03.abstract: NATO acknowledges one of its missiles accidentally hit bus in Kosovo on May 1, but insists bridge was legitimate military target; Yugoslav Foreign Ministry says attack, about 10 miles north of Pristina, capital of Kosovo, killed 47 people (S)

1999-05-03.title: CLINTON WILL MEET THE RUSSIAN ENVOY ON BALKANS TODAY
1999-05-03.abstract: Pres Clinton will meet at White House with Viktor S Chernomyrdin, Russia's envoy for Balkans, to discuss possible solutions to conflict in Kosovo; visit follows Chernomyrdin's meeting with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic and series of diplomatic moves by Milosevic, including abrupt release of three American prisoners of war, and reported request by Russian Pres Boris Yeltsin after conversation with Clinton; Chernomyrdin will also meet with UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan and leaders in London and Paris; Clinton Administration officials remain publicly resolute in determination to continue bombing of Yugoslavia and to stick with NATO's demands for ending war; Clinton says US is determined to persevere until ethnic Albanians are allowed to return to Kosovo with security and self-government; Deputy Sec of State Strobe Talbott dismisses letter from Milosevic to Clinton, sent with Rev Jesse Jackson who won release of three American soldiers, as publicity stunt; letter is said to contain suggestion for meeting of both leaders; Defense Sec William S Cohen says bombing will be intensified, despite release of soldiers; NATO spokesman Jamie Shea says Milosevic cannot expect reward for release of soldiers; ferocity of air war seems unabated as Belgrade and much of Serbia is blacked out after NATO planes strike major power plant; photo (M)

1999-05-04.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-05-04.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: CHARLES VISITS BOMB SITE -- Prince Charles visited the street in Soho, London, where a nail bomb killed three people on Friday, and offered support for minority groups, the apparent targets of three bomb attacks in April. A 22-year-old engineer, David Copeland, was arraigned, accused of murdering the three people at a gay bar in the Soho blast and of planting explosives in all three attacks.   Alan Cowell (NYT)    NORTHERN IRELAND: NEW PEACE PLAN -- A ''transitional'' government would be created for Northern Ireland with no demand for an immediate start of the disarming of paramilitary groups under a new plan to be proposed by Britain and Ireland in Belfast this week. Some weapons turnover would have to occur within six months for the government to become official under the plan, which is aimed at breaking the impasse that has blocked the carrying out of the year-old peace accord.     Warren Hoge (NYT)

1999-05-04.title: No Water, Power, Phone: A Serbian City's Trials
1999-05-04.abstract: NATO bombing of Novi Sad, Serbia, over past month has severely disrupted life of residents, forcing them to concentrate on issues of daily survival; attacks against electrical power grids in Serbia's second-largest city have plunged city into darkness and caused shortages of water; even so, few residents seem inclined to press Govt to yield to NATO's demands (M)

1999-05-04.title: Woman in the News: Mireya Elisa Moscoso; Earnest Icon For Panama
1999-05-04.abstract: Woman-in-the-News biographical profile of Mireya Elisa Moscoso, wife of late Panamanian Pres Arnulfo Arias who is first woman to be elected country's President; she has become one of few female Presidents in Latin America in what many Panamanians regard as most important election in country's short history (M)

1999-05-04.title: U.S. Frees Assets of Suspect In Embassy Terror Bombings
1999-05-04.abstract: US unblocks more than $24 million in bank accounts held by Salih Idris, Saudi owner of pharmaceutical plant in Sudan destroyed by US cruise missiles in August 1998 after terror bombings at American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania; blocked assets following intelligence reports linking Idris to Osama bib Laden, Islamic militant authorities believe orchestrated attacks (S)

1999-05-04.title: Ernest Gross, a Key Diplomat During Cold War, Dies at 92
1999-05-04.abstract: Ernest A Gross, diplomat and international lawyer who was closely involved in issues of war and peace from Korean War to decolonization and disarmament, dies at age 92; photo (M)

1999-05-05.title: Adrift Inside Yugoslavia, Trapped by Hunger and Red Tape
1999-05-05.abstract: 70,000 ethnic Albanians who were kicked out of homes in Kosovo and herded across border into Montenegro are not considered 'refugees' because they are internally displaced Yugoslav citizens; they and Montenegrin Government are in delicate position, with limits on how much help they receive from outside; many want to continue on into Albania, but UN cannot provide transportation because that could be seen as encouraging flight; more than 30,000 have been transported to main border crossing, while others are camping in various places; photo; numbers are far smaller than 350,000 in Albania, but they place heavy burden on shaky Government already under threat from Yugoslav army (M)

1999-05-05.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-05-05.abstract: EUROPE    CORSICA: FRENCH OFFICIAL DISMISSED -- In a growing political scandal, Prime Minister Lionel Jospin dismissed Bernard Bonnet, the highest French administrative official on Corsica. He also ordered the dissolution of an anti-terror squad set up after separatists on the island assassinated the official's predecessor last year. Mr. Bonnet, left, was held for questioning after three officers of the squad said that on orders from their superiors they had burned down a restaurant thought to belong to nationalist sympathizers.   Craig R. Whitney (NYT)    BRITAIN: MOVE TO BLOCK RACISTS -- Britain moved to block racist and other extremist groups from being elected to a new London assembly, just days after the capital was hit by three nail-bomb attacks on ethnic and gay communities. The Commons approved a Government amendment to draft legislation setting up the assembly, which will be elected in May 2000, requiring any party trying to win a seat in the 25-member body to gain at least 5 percent of the vote.   (Reuters)

1999-05-05.title: U.N. Nominee Is Cleared After Inquiry By State Dept.
1999-05-05.abstract: State Department inspector general Jacquelyn L Williams-Bridgers clears Richard C Holbrooke, Pres Clinton's nominee as UN ambassador, after investigation of speaking fees Holbrooke accepted while working as unpaid White House envoy to Balkans; Sen Jesse Helms reportedly will still not permit nomination to advance further until Senate gets internal documents from Justice Department about earlier ethics investigation of Holbrooke (S)

1999-05-05.title: Leaders of NATO Reject Proposal by General Clark to Fire on Ships That Defy Oil Embargo
1999-05-05.abstract: NATO's political leaders overrule Gen Wesley Clark's proposal for warships to fire on tankers that defy oil blockade against Yugoslavia; many countries are honoring blockade, but oil continues to arrive in Yugoslavia by sea; Greek-owned tanker reportedly defies ban at Montenegrin port controlled by Yugoslav Navy; Russian shipments also noted; NATO presses intense air attacks, striking 81 targets; US jet downs MIG-29 after short air-to-air confrontation; B-52s for first time attack Yugoslav airfield with bombs that are not laser- or satellite-guided; NATO denies causing destruction of second bus in Kosovo (M)

1999-05-05.title: Kosovo's Ravaged Capital Staggers Back to Half Life
1999-05-05.abstract: Serbs and a few Albanians are trickling back to Pristina, so that Kosovo capital now displays imitation of ordinary life, as long as one does not focus on empty, looted Albanian districts, bomb damage and nearly total absence of children; photos; map; city has had little bombing for month, some cafes have reopened during day, there is coffee, electricity and usually running water, but also lines for bread and no gasoline for private sale; NATO bombs echo day and night; some Albanians remain at home while sympathetic Serbian neighbors shop for them or make Serbian nameplates for their doors; others continue to flee; worst rampages seem over for now, and one Serbian official says Serbs will feel shame later on for what they have done; he describes 'catastrophe' in Pec and Podujevo, where 'vile and angry people' were 'out of control'; authorities in Pristina, advertising return of sanity, say 350 Serbian civilians, police and soldiers have been arrested for crimes like looting and arson (M)

1999-05-06.title: Businessman Slain at Border In Carjacking
1999-05-06.abstract: Jiro Sayama, Japanese businessman who commutes from US each day to work at factory on Mexican side of border, is shot to death in Tijuana in attempted carjacking; Mexican police arrest suspects Hugo Buenrostro Ramirez, 15, and Jorge Roman Frausto, 21 (M)

1999-05-06.title: World Briefing
1999-05-06.abstract: EUROPE    CORSICA: FRANCE PICKS NEW AIDE  Seeking to defuse a political crisis, the governing Socialist Party appointed Jean-Pierre Lacroix, 56, as the new top administrator for Corsica. Mr. Lacroix, who held a similar position in the Val d'Oise region north of Paris, succeeds Bernard Bonnet, who was dismissed over the firebombing of a restaurant by three members of an anti-terror squad. The officers said they were acting on orders from superiors.(Agence France-Presse)    FRANCE: MORE LANGUAGES TO BLOOM   France, signaling an end to the exclusive reign of the French language at home, will sign the European charter on regional and minority languages, Prime Minister Lionel Jospin's office said. The charter will enable regional languages to be taught in schools and used in official documents, the news media and economic, cultural and social activities. Brittany, Alsace, the Basque region and Corsica are among areas where support for local languages is strong.    (Reuters)    EUROPEAN UNION CONFIRMS PRODI  The European Parliament, meeting in Strasbourg, France, overwhelmingly approved the nomination of former Prime Minister Romano Prodi of Italy as the next president of the European Commission. The assembly voted by 392-to-72, with 41 abstentions, to endorse Mr. Prodi, left, who was chosen to succeed Jacques Santer after he and the 19 other members of the European Union's executive body resigned in March following corruption and mismanagement charges.    (Reuters)    SPAIN: PINOCHET CHARGES APPEALED  Prosecutors appealed Judge Baltasar Garzon's recent move to add 50 more cases of torture to his request for the extradition from Britain of Chile's ex-dictator, Augusto Pinochet, court officials in Madrid said. The appeal said Mr. Garzon could not broaden the case as General Pinochet was not directly implicated in crimes, the officials said. Mr. Garzon ordered General Pinochet's arrest in London last October on charges of crimes against humanity.    (Reuters)    NORTHERN IRELAND: TRIMBLE MEETS PROTESTERS  The leader of Ulster's major Protestant party, David Trimble, met for the first time with residents of Carvaghy Road in Portadown, who have long protested an annual march by the Protestant Orange Order through their neighborhood. The order's leaders refused to accompany him. The meeting came on the eve of a new round of talks in London today.    Warren Hoge (NYT)    BRITAIN: BLAIR TALKS TO MUSLIMS  Prime Minister Tony Blair acknowledged that Britain's Muslims faced widespread prejudice and urged more of them to enter politics. Mr. Blair, the first Prime Minister to address the Muslim Council of Britain, was speaking after three bomb attacks in London, one aimed at Bangladeshis near a mosque. Mr. Blair said the bombings had brought British society together and praised Islam for its racial tolerance.    (Reuters)    TURKEY: OCALAN LAWYERS THREATEN TO QUIT  Lawyers for Abdullah Ocalan have threatened to resign before the Kurdish rebel leader's trial begins May 31 unless defense conditions are improved. The lawyers demanded that the defense team be allowed to meet with Mr. Ocalan privately and that authorities stop reading their notes and files when they visit him in prison. Mr. Ocalan is accused of treason for leading a 15-year war for autonomy in Turkey's southeast. He faces the death penalty if convicted.    (AP)    THE AMERICAS    COLOMBIA: DRUG CZAR FIRED  The director of the Government's war on drugs has been dismissed after allegations in the local press of corruption, including the personal use of real estate and vehicles taken from cocaine traffickers. Ruben Olarte, appointed head of the National Drug Council eight months ago, denied any wrongdoing. His dismissal came a day after the Government ordered the arrest of Gustavo Alvarez, governor of the province that includes Cali, on charges he accepted bribes from the cocaine cartel there.    Larry Rohter (NYT)    MEXICO: PRI AIDE ACCUSED  Cuauhtemoc Gutierrez, a leader in Mexico City of the governing party known as the PRI, has been released on $10,600 bail after a night in jail on aggravated theft charges. Mr. Gutierrez was accused of organizing squatters to invade a vacant nine-acre plot in an industrial suburb. PRI leaders said the arrest was politically motivated. But the PRI has long sought to obtain votes from the urban poor by encouraging them to set up homesteads in vacant lands.    Sam Dillon (NYT)    CANADA: ONTARIO VOTE SCHEDULED  Ontario voters will head to the polls June 3 in an election called a day after the Progressive Conservative government released a budget promising billions in tax cuts and after four years of severe reductions in spending on education and welfare. Most polls show the Liberals and Conservatives tied at around 40 percent each.    (NYT)    VENEZUELA: CHARTER ELECTION JULY 25  Venezuelans will vote on July 25 to choose the 131 members of a new National Constituent Assembly to rewrite the country's Constitution, the National Electoral Council announced. The draft of the new charter will be voted on in a referendum in 2000.    (Agence France-Presse)    AFRICA    NIGERIA: NEW CONSTITUTION SIGNED  The outgoing military leader, Gen. Abdulsalami Abubakar, signed a new Constitution intended to insure a smooth transition to civilian rule. The document, to take effect when President-elect Olusegun Obasanjo takes over on May 29, is based on a civilian charter drafted in 1979. But General Abubakar, left, provided no further details aside from hinting that it would encourage a decentralized Government. No copies of the document were made available.    (AP)    RWANDA: BELGIAN REQUEST REFUSED  Rwanda refused to give up its attempt to put a former army major on trial for the murder of 10 Belgian peacekeepers in 1994, despite Belgium's requests that it be allowed to try him. The former major, Bernard Ntuyahaga, is accused of ordering the killing of Rwanda's Prime Minister and the 10 Belgians deployed to protect her.    (Reuters)    SUDAN: U.S. DECISION PRAISED  Sudan hailed as ''fair and courageous'' a U.S. decision to unfreeze the assets of Saleh Idris, owner of the pharmaceutical plant destroyed in an American missile attack last August. Junior Foreign Minister Ali Abdel Rahman Al Nimeiri, said the decision ''testifies to the falsehood of the accusations against Sudan and to the erroneous attack on the factory on grounds that it produced chemical weaons.''    (Agence France-Presse)    COMOROS: CABINET MAKEUP ANNOUNCED   Col. Azaly Assoumani, who seized power in a coup, announced plans to set up a mixed military-civilian Cabinet with himself as President, Prime Minister and Defense Minister. The Cabinet, which Colonel Azaly will choose, will include the army chief of staff, another military officer and 11 civilians.    (Agence France-Presse)    ASIA    PHILIPPINES: U.S. PACT DENOUNCED  About 2,000 Filipinos marched to the U.S. Embassy in Manila to denounce an agreement on resumption of military exercises between American and Philippine forces. The Philippine Senate is scheduled to vote within two weeks on the accord, which is opposed by leftist and church groups.    (Reuters)    AFGHANISTAN: 2 DIE IN BLAST AT BASE  At least two people were reported killed by a large blast that the Taliban Islamic Government said was caused by an opposition rocket attack on a military base in a northern suburb of Kabul. Taliban forces prevented reporters from visiting the site, some 15 miles from the front line between the Taliban and the opposition.    (Reuters)    MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: MOVE TO AID EX-MAYOR  Moderate Iranian legislators and Government officials began a last-minute campaign to save the former Mayor of Teheran, Gholamhossein Karbaschi, from jail. About 70 officials sent a letter to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei asking him to pardon Mr. Karbaschi, left, who is due to begin serving a two-year jail term today, the conservative newspaper Kayhan reported. Mr. Karbaschi, 44, a leading reformer close to President Mohammad Khatami, has been free since he was convicted last year on corruption charges.    (Agence France-Presse)     Compiled by Terence Neilan

1999-05-06.title: Supplies for Mexico's Needy Sit as Donor and Officials Argue
1999-05-06.abstract: School bus loaded with wheelchairs, aspirin and pencils for rebel Indian villages in Chiapas has been blocked for three weeks from crossing from Texas into Mexico, tangled in suspicions between Government and foreign aid donors who want to help anti-Government groups in troubled state (M)

1999-05-06.title: Hints Seen That Milosevic Is Readying His People for a Deal to Resolve the Conflict
1999-05-06.abstract: Local analysts and journalists in Belgrade say they detect small signs that Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic is preparing public opinion for end to war over Kosovo and for deal with West (M)

1999-05-06.title: U.S. Suspends Sale of Handguns to Venezuela
1999-05-06.abstract: Clinton Administration suspends sales of handguns to Venezuelan gun dealers, concerned that many of guns are ending up in hands of narcotics gangs and guerrilla groups in neighboring Colombia (M)

1999-05-07.title: Milosevic Optimistic for 'Fair Agreement'
1999-05-07.abstract: Pres Slobodan Milosevic is quoted as saying that he believes 'fair agreement' on Kosovo could be reached in negotiations with West, ending bombing and allowing return of at least some refugees; photo with Karolos Papoulias, former Greek Foreign Minister (M)

1999-05-07.title: RUSSIA IN ACCORD ON NEED FOR FORCE TO PATROL KOSOVO -- After Pact, New Issues
1999-05-07.abstract: RUSSIA IN ACCORD ON NEED FOR FORCE TO PATROL KOSOVO** News analysis of Clinton Administration's enlisting Russia to its side in Kosovo conflict; in so doing, United States and allies, calculating that isolating Pres Slobodan Milosevic is critical to setting stage for negotiations, finesse several of their key demands and make it clear that there is room to maneuver on composition of international security force; today's statement in Germany is intended to set stage for what is known as United Nations Chapter 7 resolution, which authorizes military operations in a country without its consent (M)

1999-05-07.title: Lawmakers, Backing Bill For Kosovo, Add Billions
1999-05-07.abstract: House votes to spend billions of dollars for NATO bombing campaign in Yugoslavia that it declined to endorse days ago; Republican leaders vow to pour billions more into a military they contend Pres Clinton had gutted; House, rewriting deeply mixed message it sent on battle in Balkans last week, approves Clinton's $6 billion request to finance air war and to aid refugees; adds almost $7 billion in Pentagon programs, many of which bear no direct relation to fighting in Balkans; Vote on $13 billion spending bill is 311 to 105 (M)

1999-05-07.title: NATO Says Bombing Has Serb Forces Pinned Down and Losing Equipment
1999-05-07.abstract: NATO says that its bombing has destroyed half Serbian army's ammunition stores in Kosovo and 200 tanks and other armored vehicles, artillery pieces and trucks--about 20 percent of all Serbian heavy equipment in province; maps show concentrations of Serb forces in Kosovo (M)

1999-05-07.title: Macedonian Troops Said to Push 1,000 Refugees Back
1999-05-07.abstract: Officials from office of United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees accuse Macedonian soldiers of pushing as many as 1,000 ethnic Albanian refugees back into Kosovo; Macedonian border guards effectively seal border, allowing no refugees to cross into Macedonia only two days after 5,000 ethnic Albanians flee there from Kosovo; photo of border crossing (M)

1999-05-08.title: Clinton Seeing Bosnian Model For Kosovo Use
1999-05-08.abstract: Pres Clinton describes peacekeeping operation in Bosnia as model for force envisioned to return to protect ethnic Albanian refugees in Kosovo; says arrangement, which includes American soldiers working under direction of NATO with UN approval, is good model since it involves Russian and Ukranian participation; asserts, with NATO officials, that alliance will have role in peacekeeping force despite absence of such language in communique issued by Group of Seven and Russia; although Russians agreed to communique's call for international force in Kosovo, they remain opposed to involvement of NATO, in part because Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic opposes it (M)

1999-05-08.title: Among Allies, Doubts and Disagreement on Oil Embargo
1999-05-08.abstract: France is thought to have persuaded most NATO allies that there is no basis in international law for allied warships to challenge ships of countries that have not declared war against Yugoslavia; this has led allied diplomats to conclude that NATO's planned naval blockade of oil deliveries to Yugoslavia may not have desired effect (M)

1999-05-08.title: China Cracks Down on Foreign Satellite Transmissions
1999-05-08.abstract: Chinese Government clamps down on illegal reception of foreign television channels; industry executives say crackdown appears to be latest in periodic enforcement of laws that restrict satellite-delivered television services to only hotels and residential complexes for foreigners; in practice, Government has rarely enforced laws; now, with 10th anniversary of Tiananmen Square massacre less than month away, some industry experts believe Govenrment wants to curtail foreign channels that might carry news reports about event (M)

1999-05-08.title: Milosevic Aide on Kosovo Urges Albanians to Return
1999-05-08.abstract: Zoran Andjelkovic, Serbian minister for youth and sport, says he is doing his best to hold Kosovo together and to promote reconciliation among all Serbian province's ethnic groups, interview; describes his efforts to get aid to those who need it and to encourage ethnic Albanian refugees to return home; denies charges of 'ethnic cleansing' and claims no one left Kosovo before NATO bombing started; says burning of houses and shops of Albanians all over Kosovo is work of multiethnic criminal gangs; other senior Yugoslav and Serbian officials privately acknowledge accuracy of some refugee accounts of Serbian paramilitaries' forcing them to leave Kosovo, but they also insist army and police are reining in paramilitaries (M)

1999-05-08.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-05-08.abstract: COMBAT    * NATO warplanes hit the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade. China said two people were killed. Its United Nations envoy condemned the attack and called for an emergency meeting of the Security Council.  * Cluster bombs hit an outdoor market and hospital complex in Yugoslavia's third-largest city, Nis, killing 15 people and wounding 70. NATO said the bombs missed their target.

1999-05-09.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-05-09.abstract: Update of military situation in Kosovo, Serbia (S)

1999-05-09.title: Syrian President Positions Son as His Successor
1999-05-09.abstract: Pres Hafez al-Assad of Syria, 70, reportedly seeks to smooth way for succession of his son, Bashar; suffers from variety of physical ailments, looks increasingly frail and is rarely seen in public; son, British-trained opthalmologist, returned to Syria in 1994 after death of brother, then heir apparent, in automobile accident; photos; map (M)

1999-05-09.title: John Howard Is Dead at 86; British Hero of D-Day Invasion
1999-05-09.abstract: Maj John Howard, commander of glider-borne British infantrymen who seized stategically vital Pegasus Bridge in first battle of D-Day invasion of Normandy, dies at age 86; photo (M)

1999-05-09.title: Help for Vast African Refugee Crisis May Suffer in Kosovo's Shadow, Aid Groups Say
1999-05-09.abstract: International aid officials fear Balkan refugee crisis may shift attention away from plight of refugees across Africa, notably in Congo, where thousands fled to escape civil war and are stuck at border between Congo and Zambia; Africa is continent with largest number of refugees, estimated at 7.3 million; photos (M)

1999-05-09.title: Chinese Evacuate a Bombed Embassy in Serbia
1999-05-09.abstract: Chinese Embassy personnel evacuate their bombed embassy in Belgrade, still dazed and shaken after NATO raid hit building; inside compound, stress and horror are plain; at least three people die, and about 20 injured; photo (M)

1999-05-10.title: Tightrope for China
1999-05-10.abstract: By depicting the United States and NATO as evil aggressors in Yugoslavia, China's leaders may have unleashed forces that will come back to haunt them. News analysis, page A8.

1999-05-10.title: Pope's Visit Adds to Hope Of a Future With the West
1999-05-10.abstract: Pope John Paul II's three-day visit to Romania gives country an opportunity to bask in rare international attention and make case to West that it belongs in Europe; Romania was bitterly disappointed that its applications to begin negotiations to join NATO and European Union were rejected; Government supports NATO in war with Yugoslavia, has opened its airspace to NATO forces and is complying with oil embargo against Belgrade; its support for NATO is uneasy position for country that shares border and Orthodox faith with Serbs and has simmering ethnic tensions of its own; photo (M)

1999-05-10.title: Reports Show Scientist Gave U.S. Radar Secrets to Chinese
1999-05-10.abstract: Court records and Government documents show that a scientist working on classified Pentagon project in 1997 provided China with secrets about advanced radar technology being developed to track submarines; show information about promising, top-secret radar technology which has been in development for past two decades was divulged to Chinese nuclear-weapons experts during lecture in Beijing in May 1997 by Peter Lee, scientists who worked for TRW Inc, which had been hired by Pentagon; law-enforcement officials say Federal prosecutors wanted to charge Lee with espionage but were unable to, in part because Navy officials in Washington would not permit testimony about technology in open court; Lee ended up pleading guilty to filing false statement about 1997 trip to China and to divulging classified laser data to Chinese scientists during earlier trip in 1985 (M)

1999-05-10.title: Embassy Attack Followed By Defiance Toward NATO
1999-05-10.abstract: Bombing of Chinese Embassy in Belgrade, Serbia, by NATO warplanes has convinced many residents that they are being deliberately targeted by NATO and has hardened their determination to resist; many residents believe Embassy was deliberately attacked (M)

1999-05-10.title: South Africans Mock an Anti-Corruption Effort
1999-05-10.abstract: Corruption is emerging as key political issue in South Africa's second post-apartheid elections; majority of South Africans give Pres Nelson Mandela's governing African National Congress poor rating in rooting out and controlling corruption within its ranks, many deriding its efforts as insulting and insincere; ANC has lately begun to take on issue; photo (M)

1999-05-11.title: WORLD NEWS BRIEFS
1999-05-11.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    ISRAEL: P.L.O. OFFICES ORDERED CLOSED -- The police served orders to close three offices in Orient House, the P.L.O. headquarters in East Jerusalem, but the action was delayed pending a petition to the Supreme Court by Israeli peace advocates. Palestinians called the order an election ploy by Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyhau, who has made Jerusalem a centerpiece of his campaign. Faisal Husseini, left, the top Palestinian official in East Jerusalem, warned that a forcible closing could set off riots.     Joel Greenberg (NYT)    IRAN: PARDON URGED FOR EX-MAYOR -- A majority of Iranian legislators have signed a petition calling for a pardon for the former Mayor of Teheran, Gholamhossein Karbaschi, a moderate who began serving a two-year prison term last week on corruption charges.   (Agence France-Presse)

1999-05-11.title: Tokyo Journal; Vying for Korean Hearts: Kim Il Sung vs. Hip-Hop
1999-05-11.abstract: 650,000 ethnic Koreans in Japan, by far Japan's largest minority, seem to be at crossroads and are increasingly choosing Japanese traditions rather than Korean; after half century in which Korean population in Japan fought constantly with its hosts, it is becoming more subdued; each year more than 10,000 Koreans are taking Japanese citizenship; photo (M)

1999-05-11.title: NATO TO HIT SERBS FROM 2 MORE SIDES
1999-05-11.abstract: NATO will open two new fronts in bombing campaign against Yugoslavia; will launch air strikes from Turkey and Hungary in effort to surround Yugoslavia militarily and further isolate it politically; attacks have thus far been mainly from bases in Western Europe and US and from aircraft carriers in Adriatic; aim is to increase political pressure on Pres Slobodan Milosevic and to help in countering Serbia's battered but still resilient air defenses; decision is based on effort to find new bases for hundreds of additional planes requested for air campaign by Supreme NATO Comdr Gen Wesley K Clark; photo (M)

1999-05-11.title: Turkey Has Tight Rein on Kurd Center
1999-05-11.abstract: Profile of Lice, town in eastern Turkey where Kurdistan Workers Party was founded in 1978; it remains symbol of Kurdish militancy despite attempts of Turkish Army to destroy it in quest to eradicate Kurdish guerrilla insurgency; photos; map (M)

1999-05-11.title: Belgrade Says Pullout In Kosovo Has Begun
1999-05-11.abstract: Yugoslav Army reports completing operations against insurgent Kosovo Liberation Army and is beginning partial withdrawal of military and police forces from Kosovo; says once agreement is reached with UN on international peacekeeping force, forces will be reduced to peace-time levels; NATO officials say bombing will continue; Sec of State Madeleine K Albright calls Yugoslavia's announcement 'half measure;' says air war will continue until all of NATO's demands are met (M)

1999-05-12.title: Dependent On U.S. Now, Europe Vows Defense Push
1999-05-12.abstract: Defense and foreign ministers of Western European Union, Europe's long-dormant military arm, vow to develop strong common military policy in face of their dependence on United States in NATO's military campaign against Serbia, meeting, Bremen, Germany; union is made up of 10 European nations that belong to NATO; Germany and several other European governments are pushing to quickly merge it with European Union (M)

1999-05-12.title: Energy Secretary Outlines Plan to Improve Nuclear Security
1999-05-12.abstract: Energy Sec Bill Richardson, vowing to assert direct control over Government's fiercely independent nuclear weapons laboratories, sets wide-ranging program to strengthen security after charges that China repeatedly got nuclear secrets from labs; plans to name new Energy Department 'security czar;' holds labs' independence and ability to exploit informal political channels to get money directly from Congress have enabled them to ignore department recommendations on improving security and counter-intelligence and screening foreign visitors (M)

1999-05-12.title: U.N. Aide to Meet Milosevic, Angering Some
1999-05-12.abstract: Mary Robinson, United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, plans to meet Pres Slobodan Milosevic to protest Yugoslavia's human rights abuses, plan that surprises and upsets some diplomats; Sec Gen Kofi Annan strongly backs plan; Human Rights Watch and Jacob Blaustein Institute for Advancement of Human Rights ask Robinson to cancel meeting, joint letter (M)

1999-05-12.title: Court Blocks Closing of 3 Palestinian Offices
1999-05-12.abstract: Israeli Supreme Court bars Government from closing three offices at Palestinian political headquarters in East Jerusalem, easing crisis; order is political blow to Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu, who pressed for closing as part of re-election drive; strengthens Faisal al-Husseini, top Palestinian leader in East Jerusalem, as Palestinians rally around headquarters, which they consider symbol of their claims to city (M)

1999-05-12.title: China Students Are Caught Up By Nationalism
1999-05-12.abstract: Chinese students are awash in nationalism and anti-Americanism spurred by United States bombing of China's Belgrade, Serbia, Embassy; are venting fury many Chinese say they feel; anti-US demonstrations lead some students to boycott American goods and drop plans for study abroad; photo (M)

1999-05-13.title: Allies Detail Busiest Bombing Yet, but See Clash Continuing
1999-05-13.abstract: Senior NATO and Pentagon officials say that after most intense 24 hours of bombing so far, NATO has weakened Yugoslavia's forces but still not broken their ability to fight; Gen Henry H Shelton, Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, says attacks have exacted significant toll on Serb forces in Kosovo and throughout rest of Yugoslavia; Pres Slobodan Milosevic says NATO attack has caused deaths of 'many members' of Yugoslav army (M)

1999-05-13.title: Naval Officer Is Nominated Ambassador To Beijing
1999-05-13.abstract: White House officials say Pres Clinton plans to nominate Adm Joseph W Prueher to be next Ambassador to Beijing; say Administration hopes that nomination will signal importance of working with China on critical national security issues that have divided two countries; Administration ends six-month search and finally selects replacement for departing Amb James Sasser in same week that NATO warplanes mistakenly bombed Chinese Embassy in Belgrade, sparking demonstrations throughout China (M)

1999-05-13.title: 5th Term Is Opposed for Head of Weather Agency
1999-05-13.abstract: Contentious campaign is under way over who will head World Meteorological Organization, United Nations agency that tries to predict global weather disasters; Godwin Olu Patrick Obasi of Nigeria is seeking new four-year term at agency, which he has headed for 16 years; he is opposed by Manuel B Dengo of Costa Rica and Gordon A McBean of Canada; delegates from 185 member countries will select leader on May 14 (M)

1999-05-13.title: Yeltsin Warns West It Must Accept Russia's Proposals on Kosovo : Moscow Shake-Up Clouds Peace Effort
1999-05-13.abstract: WASHINGTON—  Diplomatic efforts to resolve the Kosovo crisis were plunged into uncertainty Wednesday by the dismissal of the Russian prime minister, Yevgeni Primakov, one day after China and Russia demanded that NATO halt the air campaign against Yugoslavia.

1999-05-13.title: Trade War Looms Over Hormone Beef Ban as EU Reiterates Health Fears
1999-05-13.abstract: BRUSSELS—  The European Union is poised to set off a trade war by ignoring a Thursday deadline set by the World Trade Organization to lift its 10-year ban on foreign beef produced with growth hormones.

1999-05-14.title: With Prospects Bleaker, Netanyahu Lashes Out
1999-05-14.abstract: Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu fights for his political survival in Israel, with only ultra-Orthodox Jewish community standing nearly unanimously by his side; his candidacy is bleeding support from other core constituencies: Sephardic Jews, secular right-wingers and Russian immigrants; many observers believe his behavior is panicky, as though he is on the ropes; in recent appearances, he has lashed out at the left, the elite, the media and polls; Netanyahu is trailing his main opponent, Ehud Barak, by solid 8 points in latest polls; some conservative analysts now suggest that Barak, Labor Party leader, could win clear majority in May 17 vote, obviating need for anticipated runoff on June 1; photos (L)

1999-05-14.title: Clinton Blames Milosevic, Not Fate, for Bloodshed
1999-05-14.abstract: Pres Clinton says that he has misread Balkan history, and apologizes for any suggestions that there is 'some Balkan disease' that condemns all sides in Yugoslavia to endless cycle of ethnic blood feuds, speech to group of World War II combat veterans in Washington; Clinton tries to paint compelling portrait of atrocities inside Kosovo, evoking images of Hitler's slaughter of Jews and detailing eyewitness accounts of mass executions, rapes and people being set on fire; charges that Pres Slobodan Milosevic of Yugoslavia has been catalyst for current ethnic cleansing (M)

1999-05-14.title: Milosevic Refuses to See Top U.N. Rights Official
1999-05-14.abstract: Mary Robinson, United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, is frustrated in her aim to ask Pres Slobodan Milosevic of Yugoslavia about his Government's human rights record when he refuses to see her in Belgrade (S)

1999-05-14.title: Clinton's Remarks in Defense of Military Intervention in Balkans
1999-05-14.abstract: Excerpts from Pres Clinton's speech to a veterans' group on Kosovo (M)

1999-05-14.title: Phnom Penh Journal; Intrepid Lawyer Rises for First Khmer Rouge Trial
1999-05-14.abstract: Benson Samay, Cambodian lawyer, comments on his decision to defend Ta Mok, Khmer Rouge leader to be tried in deaths of more than a million people from 1975 to 1979; Ta Mok's selection of Samay remains puzzling to several experts; Samay, who has no law degree, represents Hun Sen's governing Cambodian People's Party and has been an adviser to top officials; photos (M)

1999-05-15.title: Gaiman Journal; Just Think of It as a Little Wales With Cactuses
1999-05-15.abstract: Gaiman, Argentina, has become beacon for Welsh immigrants from British Isles and US, ever since colonists first migrated in 1865 and kept arriving until eve of World War I; their goal was to gain some measure of independence from Britain, to speak own Celtic language without interference and let their identity bloom; have established lasting cultural enclave with roots deep enough to defend it against time and assimilation; map; photo (M)

1999-05-15.title: World Briefing
1999-05-15.abstract: AFRICA    BURUNDI: CONVICTIONS IN PRESIDENT'S DEATH -- The Supreme Court condemned 5 people to death and convicted 23 others for the killing of Melchior Ndadaye, Burundi's first democratically elected President, in 1993. Mr. Ndadaye, a Hutu, was shot by Tutsi troops shortly after his inauguration, unleashing ethnic strife that killed more than 200,000 Burundians. All the accused, who are mostly Tutsi, pleaded not guilty.   (AP)    GUINEA-BISSAU: NEW PRESIDENT SWORN IN -- Malan Bacai Sanha, 52, left, the former head of Parliament, was sworn in, replacing Joao Bernardo Vieira, who remained in hiding in the Portuguese Embassy. The military leaders who ousted him last week demanded that he be handed over for trial.   (Reuters)

1999-05-15.title: ALBANIANS KILLED AS KOSOVO VILLAGE IS BLASTED APART
1999-05-15.abstract: More than 60 Albanians die and scores are badly wounded as bombs rip apart village of Korisa, Serbia; Yugoslav Government says attacks were carried out by NATO warplanes; Russia condemns attack; victims are reportedly part of group of about 500 ethnic Albanians traveling through southwest Kosovo and stopping in Korisa for night; photos; map (M)

1999-05-15.title: Yugoslavia Fears Loss of Its E-Mail
1999-05-15.abstract: Internet users in Belgrade fear being cut off from World Wide Web as result of Pres Clinton's updated Executive Order intended to prevent American companies from dealing in goods, services and technology ith Yugoslavia; Administration says it seeks to cut commerce, but not flow of information to Yugoslavia (S)

1999-05-15.title: First Lady Pays Visit To a Camp
1999-05-15.abstract: Hillary Rodham Clinton makes carefully orchestrated visit to Macedonia, illustrating Washington's continuing struggle to balance diverse strands of its Kosovo policy; she commiserates with ethnic Albanians at refugee camp, but goes out of her way to avoid offending Macedonian sensibilities; ethnic Macedonians generally oppose NATO's bombing and fear their country will be overrun with fleeing ethnic Albanians; Clinton seeks to defuse anti-American sentiment and to bolster Macedonia's fragile coalition Govermment (M)

1999-05-16.title: Colombia Rebels Reign in Ceded Area
1999-05-16.abstract: Marxist guerrillas in Colombia have taken over swath of jungle and savanna around San Vicente del Caguan, region ceded by Government to draw guerrillas into talks on ending 35-year insurgency; guerrillas, who have effectively collected taxes in area for years, are cementing control and taking steps to insure they cannot be expelled if process breaks down; photo; map; they have expelled Government prosecutor and demanded Catholic Church remove two priests who object to rebels' behavior (M)

1999-05-16.title: Vietnam Sees War's Legacy in Its Young
1999-05-16.abstract: North Vietnam blames Agent Orange used by US forces during war for mental handicaps and physical deformities of thousands of children whose fathers were exposed years earlier while fighting for North Vietnam; absence of US aid for deformed children still rankles with many Vietnamese, even though evidence is often anecdotal; one diplomat observes that issue is driven by propaganda, and Vietnam blames Agent Orange for 'every deformed baby' (M)

1999-05-16.title: China Picks Local Banker After Portugal Cedes Macao
1999-05-16.abstract: Macao gets first Chinese leader as Portugal's 442-year rule draws to close; committee appointed by Beijing selects local banker, Edmund Ho, to administercolony after it reverts to Chinese rule in December; Ho, 44-year-old scion of leading Chinese family, is de facto leader of Macao's Chinese business community and has longstanding ties to Chinese Government (M)

1999-05-16.title: Mexico Measures Identity in Dollars
1999-05-16.abstract: Mexico and other Latin American countries debate emotional implications of proposals that they adopt US dollar; economists say dollar's stability would do much to cure chronic inflation and volatility of Latin economies, especially in Mexico, but leaders are reluctant to give up policy powers and sense of national identity that go along with trading in their own money (S)

1999-05-16.title: Blair Sets New Deadline for an Ulster Government
1999-05-16.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair sets June 30 deadline for Northern Ireland parties to break deadlock and establish new power-sharing government called for in peace accord; main obstacle is Ulster Unionists' refusal to permit Sinn Fein, political arm of IRA, to assume two positions in ten-member Assembly executive unless IRA begins to turn over weapons (S)

1999-05-17.title: U.S. Orders 2 Yugoslavs To Be Freed As P.O.W.'s
1999-05-17.abstract: Pres Clinton reportedly orders release of two Yugoslav soldiers captured in Kosovo; both are being held at American base in Mannheim, Germany; NATO reports attacking Serbian artillery and armor in southern and eastern Kosovo; says weather hampered pilots; voice determination to continue air war despite regrettable civilian casualties (M)

1999-05-17.title: In Subtle Ways, Yugoslav Republic Guards Against Serb-Led Coup
1999-05-17.abstract: Govt officials of Yugoslav Republic of Montenegro are playing cat-and-mouse game with Yugoslavia's Second Army, which they suspect of plotting coup in alliance with powerful segment of Montenegro's parliamentary opposition that supports Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; situation has reportedly reached extremely delicate balance between civil war and uneasy peace; officials say Yugoslav Army is acting as army of occupation, ignoring Montenegro's Constitution and imposing order of its own; photo (M)

1999-05-17.title: 2 CANDIDATES QUIT IN ISRAEL ON DAY BEFORE ELECTIONS
1999-05-17.abstract: Yitzhak Mordechai and Benny Begin withdraw from election contest for Prime Minister in stunning turn of events day before May 17 Israeli election, probably paving way for conclusive victory by Labor Party candidate Ehud Barak over incumbent Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu; Mordechai, former Defense Minister and leader of Center Party, endorses Barak; says Netanyahu must be defeated; Benny Begin, leader of ultra-nationalist coalition who felt Netanyahu betrayed supporters by turning over West Bank land to Palestinians, declines to throw support to Netanyahu; their withdrawal gives Barak 10-point lead in public-opinion polls; photos (M)

1999-05-17.title: Milosevic Is Said to Be Ready For Deal but Not Humiliation
1999-05-17.abstract: Yugoslav officials and analysts say Pres Slobodan Milosevic, after more than 50 days of NATO air raids and with troops still entrenched in Kosovo, is prepared for settlement as long as process can be portrayed as negotiation and not capitulation; he is reportedly frustrated by slow pace of mediating effort by Russian special envoy, Viktor Chernomyrdin, adding to Belgrade's already deep distrust of Moscow's intentions; consensus is that Milosevic has political room to maneuver over Kosovo and to redefine almost any negotiated outcome as victory to preserve his own grip on power; photo (M)

1999-05-17.title: Refugees Tell of 100 Civilians Massacred by Serbian Forces in Rebel Strongholds
1999-05-17.abstract: Kosovar refugees arriving in Macedonia say Serb forces killed more than 100 civilians in three villages in Drenica region of Kosovo, birthplace of Kosovo Liberation Army; map; photo (M)

1999-05-18.title: World Briefing
1999-05-18.abstract: ASIA    NORTH KOREA: AMERICAN MISSION -- Former Defense Secretary William J. Perry will travel to Pyongyang by the end of the month in what could be the first meeting between a Presidential envoy and top North Korean leaders. The State Department declined to confirm the mission, which was announced by South Korea, but a senior U.S. diplomat said it could be announced soon. The State Department said the U.S. was sending the North another 400,000 metric tons of food aid, just ahead of a planned first inspection on Wednesday of a suspected nuclear site.   David E. Sanger (NYT)

1999-05-18.title: Weining Journal; Habitat Nearly Full Leaves People's Lives Empty
1999-05-18.abstract: Chinese Govt is locked in dispute with farmers living around Cao Hai Lake in Guizhou Province that underscores mounting worldwide problem of competition between people and wildlife for scarce natural resources; large and growing population of desperately poor farmers wish to convert rich marshes into farm plots; Govt has declared lake and its surroundings, crucial wintering ground for dozens of species of migratory waterfowl, protected reserve; photo; map (M)

1999-05-18.title: ISRAELIS CHOOSE A NEW LEADER AND REMAKE THEIR PARLIAMENT
1999-05-18.abstract: Israelis overwhelmingly elect Labor Party leader Ehud Barak as new Prime Minister in landslide vote, rejecting tumultuous leadership of Benjamin Netanyahu whose galvanizing character dominated Israel for three years; Barak captures 56 percent of vote, compared with 43.9 for Netanyahu; election of protege of late Yitzhak Rabin is seen as resounding call for revival of languishing peace effort between Israelis and Palestinians; Barak, in victory speech, cites need to strengthen country's security by moving forward to peace agreements; issues call for unity, and vows to be everyone's Prime Minister; Netanyahu concedes defeat and steps down as leader of conservative Likud Party; cordially congratulates Barak; Palestinians celebrate his demise; Yasir Arafat calls and congratulates Barak; Pres Clinton speak with Netanyahu; praises him and thanks him for his hard work; election, which vanquished right wing with new center comprised of four new parties, drastically alters balance of power; affords Barak luxury of assembling large bloc of secular parties without ultra-Orthodox representatives, who appear to have lost exclusive role as as swing vote between left and right; photos (M)

1999-05-18.title: KOSOVO UPDATE
1999-05-18.abstract: Update of situation in Kosovo, Serbia

1999-05-18.title: Gandhi as Premier? Her Party's in Turmoil
1999-05-18.abstract: Sonia Gandhi, Italian-born widow who inherited mantle of India's founding political dynasty following assassination of husband Rajiv Gandhi in 1991, resigns as leader of Congress Party following revolt by three party leaders against her candidacy for Prime Minister because of her foreign birth and lack of political experience; party's shaken governing body promptly rejects resignation and moves to dissuade her from stepping down; say she has single-handedly rescued party from disastrous decline during past year; Gandhi became Indian citizen in 1983; photo (M)

1999-05-19.title: The White House, Buoyed by Political Change in Israel, Pushes to Revive Peace Talks
1999-05-19.abstract: Pres Clinton loses little time in trying to capitalize on victory of Ehud Barak in Israeli election for Prime Minister to invigorate stalled Middle East peace talks; Clinton, who congratulated Barak by phone on May 17, speaks with King Abdullah of Jordan, Pres Hosni Mubarak of Egypt and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat (M)

1999-05-19.title: Stepashin Is Pressing Parliament On Reforms
1999-05-19.abstract: Acting Prime Min Sergei V Stepashin tells Russian Parliament that he would demand vote of confidence in his new Government if lawmakers refused to enact economic reforms on which Western lenders have conditioned billions of dollars in new loans (M)

1999-05-19.title: Despite Party's Entreaties, Gandhi Insists She's Out of Politics
1999-05-19.abstract: Sonia Gandhi refuses pleas of senior Congress Party leaders that she change her mind about resigning as president of party; Gandhi, who married into India's dominant political family and was widowed when her husband, Rajiv, was assassinated eight years ago, says some powerful figures in party do not want her to be Prime Minister because she was born in Italy and lacks experience; entire top echelon of party resign and say they would return only if she relented (M)

1999-05-19.title: NATO SAYS SERBS, FEARING LAND WAR, DIG IN ON BORDER
1999-05-19.abstract: Yugoslavia, anticipating that NATO may eventually mount ground offensive, has stationed army brigades along likely attack routes from Macedonia and Albania and is trying to fortify border; positioning of Serbian forces represents marked shift in Yugoslavia's military strategy from overseeing expulsion of ethnic Albanians from Kosovo to preparing for long battle, and focusing on blocking incursions by NATO troops as well as rebel fighters of Kosovo Liberation Army; it also signals that while two months of allied air strikes have weakened Serbian forces, it has not broken their will; map (M)

1999-05-19.title: On the Shaken Right, a First-Day Challenge
1999-05-19.abstract: Jewish settlers, laying down challenge to Ehud Barak day after he was elected Israel's Prime Minister, begin digging foundations for housing complex in Arab neighborhood of East Jerusalem; project, which has drawn protests from Palestinians and expressions of concern from Clinton Administration, is financed by Dr Irving Moskowitz, Orthodox Jewish millionaire from Miami who has paid for other Jewish settlement projects in East Jerusalem; photo (M)

1999-05-20.title: NATO's Battle Within: Is Leadership Missing?
1999-05-20.abstract: Michael R Gordon analysis contends that as Pres Clinton wrestles over question of dispatching combat troops to Kosovo, NATO's strategy for bringing war to successful close is starting to unravel; maintains that without clear direction from Washington, British, Germans and Italians have begun to promote publicly their separate and conflicting plans; holds that US has been unable to quiet unruly chorus because it has not made up its own mind about how to force Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic to accept allies' terms (M)

1999-05-20.title: Women Protest Draftees' Kosovo Duty
1999-05-20.abstract: Hundreds of women demonstrate in Krusevac and Aleksandrovac, Serbia, demanding that their husbands and sons be allowed home from army service in Kosovo; their pleas seem to be answered when truckloads of soldiers arrive home; NATO officials say that soldiers are deserters; map (M)

1999-05-20.title: Peace Opponent Pulls Out of Israeli Politics, Aiding Barak
1999-05-20.abstract: Benny Begin, ultranationalist son of late Menachem Begin, is quitting politics after his National Union Party wins just four seats in Parliament; decision seems to bode well for Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak and his effort to assemble broad coalition committed to negotiated peace with Palestinians and Syria; Begin photo (M)

1999-05-20.title: GERMANY'S LEADER PLEDGES TO BLOCK COMBAT ON GROUND
1999-05-20.abstract: Chancellor Gerhard Schroder says Germany will block NATO from fighting land war in Kosovo; opposes Britain's position that allies should consider ground attack if NATO bombing pounds Yugoslav forces to point where they can offer only scattered resistance; challenges official US explanation for bombing of Chinese Embassy in Belgrade and renews his demand for formal NATO inquiry into bombing; Italian Parliament passes resolution committing Government to seek halt in bombing; Schroder photo (M)

1999-05-20.title: Mandela Aide Tries to Allay Fears
1999-05-20.abstract: South African Deputy President, Thabo Mbeki, says African National Congress has no plans to change Constitution if it wins two-thirds majority in June 2 election (S)

1999-05-21.title: The Right to Hire and Fire Is Granted : Foreign Managers Get New Powers in China
1999-05-21.abstract: NANJING, China—  In a big step away from its socialist, protectionist past, China has permitted foreign control in the strategic steel industry.

1999-05-21.title: Congress Party Expels 3 Who Opposed Gandhi
1999-05-21.abstract: Congress Party expels three dissident leaders who opposed Sonia Gandhi's candidacy for Prime Minister because she was born in Italy; expulsions, particularly that of Sharad Pawar, a senior leader with a powerful base in western State of Maharashtra, are quite likely to weaken party's prospects in general election in Sept (M)

1999-05-21.title: British Official Warns U.S. Not to 'Lose Nerve'
1999-05-21.abstract: British Foreign Sec, Robin Cook, tells Congress and American news media that allies should not lose their nerve over Kosovo; says NATO should have troops and equipment ready to advance if Serbian forces collapse (S)

1999-05-21.title: 3 Options for Washington, All With Major Risks
1999-05-21.abstract: Analysis of three unpleasant options facing Clinton Administration in Serbia: pressing foward on current course, significantly increasing the air assaults and persuading its NATO allies, some of whom are growing testy, that preparations should begin for ground troops (M)

1999-05-21.title: Governing Party in Nepal Gains Majority in Parliamentary Election
1999-05-21.abstract: Political stability may have finally come to small Himalayan nation of Nepal after this week's elections; governing Nepali Congress seems on its way to parliamentarian majority, ending four and half years of fractitious coalitions that brought five governments to one of world's youngest democracies (S)

1999-05-22.title: Kosovo Insurgents Report Killing Russian Officer in Battle With Serbs
1999-05-22.abstract: Kosovo Liberation Army claims to have killed a Russian captain in battle with Serbian troops in Kosovo; rebel spokesmen say they have known for months that Russian officers and soldiers are fighting with Serbian troops, a claim that could complicate Russia's role in peace negotiations (M)

1999-05-22.title: Yugoslavs Demand a Role In U.N. Kosovo Settlement
1999-05-22.abstract: Nebojsa Vujovic, spokesman for Yugoslav Foreign Ministry, says Yugoslavia must play a central role in negotiations within United Nations to end war and bring a political settlement to Kosovo; says Yugolslavia will not permit NATO to dictate a solution; Vujovic praises diplomatic efforts of Russian special envoy Viktor S Chernomyrdin; photo (M)

1999-05-22.title: CRISIS IN THE BALKANS: Washington Memo; Clinton's Aims: Win the War, Keep the U.S. Voters Content
1999-05-22.abstract: White House officials say Pres Clinton is waging pragmatic and moral style of warfare in Yugoslavia that pushes military action as far as it can go, in view of hard limits of American public opinion, Congressional dissent and discord within NATO alliance; officials say this approach gives United States more flexibility in defending American values than all-or-nothing precepts of retired Gen Colin L Powell, who said troops should not be committed without clear objectives and military means to achieve them; officials call Clinton's policy 'coercive diplomacy'; it is not intended to crush foreign enemy, but to achieve military and political goals overseas without losing votes at home; photo (M)

1999-05-22.title: Killing in Italy Stirs Fears of a Revival of Terrorist Times Past
1999-05-22.abstract: Assassination of Massimo D'Antona, a labor adviser to Government, is sending shockwaves through Italy, especially because statement released by group claiming responsibility for killing was signed the 'Red Brigades,' same name used by ultraleft-wing organization that was responsible for dozens of terrorist attacks in 1970's and early 1980's (M)

1999-05-22.title: CLINTON IS PUSHING FOR 50,000 TROOPS AT KOSOVO BORDER
1999-05-22.abstract: Clinton Administration says United States will press its allies to move quickly to deploy peacekeeping force of 50,000 troops on borders of Kosovo; such a force would include 7,000 Americans; NATO is expected to take up issue in next week; troops are intended to help return Kosovar Albanian refugees and to police any agreement reached with Serbs; NATO and Pentagon leave open possibility that troops could enter Kosovo without permission of Pres Slobodan Milosevic; in that sense, Pentagon's announcement of support for such a force represents effort to put more pressure on Milosevic; US is said to be negotiating with Russia on concessions that would allow Milosevic to keep some forces in Kosovo; that would represent significant shift from key NATO condition for peace accord: that Milosevic agree to remove his soldiers and police forces from Kosovo; photo (M)

1999-05-23.title: Another Bomb Error
1999-05-23.abstract: NATO bombed an important Kosovo Liberation Army base, thinking it was still in the hands of Yugoslav troops. The Kosovo rebels said 7 fighters were killed and 25 wounded.  Article, page 10.

1999-05-23.title: Tracking Suspicions About China's Atom Spying
1999-05-23.abstract: Release of Congressional investigation report on China's suspected theft of United States nuclear technology is likely to alter US national security and foreign policy, slow the export of technology and reshape Clinton Adminstration's commercial diplomacy toward China; investigating panel, led by Repr Christopher Cox, has reportedly concluded, secretly and unanimously, that China has been systematically stealing information on US nuclear warheads and missiles from 1970's until today; panel was created by former Speaker Newt Gingrich to look into possible misconduct in Clinton Administration policy of encouraging commercial exports to China, but took turn to become decade's deepest look into post-cold war espionage after security leak at Los Alamos nuclear laboratory was revealed (M)

1999-05-23.title: A City's Exiles Recall How Good They Had It
1999-05-23.abstract: Bajram Curri, Albanian neighborhood in Prizren, Kosovo's second-largest city, is fondly and painfully remembered by residents who have fled Serbian aggression or the fear of it and are now refugees in Kukes, Albania; photo; maps (M)

1999-05-23.title: Olivetti Faces Risky Future By Acquiring Telecom Italia
1999-05-23.abstract: Olivetti embarks on a risky business gamble in seizing control of Telecom Italia, a deal that will reshuffle the European telephone landscape; most far-reaching consequence of deal may be its agreement to cede its telephone activities to Mannesmann AG of Germany, its present phone partner; Mannesmann will inherit virtually all of Olivetti's capable phone business management, while only Roberto Colaninno, Olivetti's chief executive, and possibly a handful of close associates will pass to Telecom Italia (M)

1999-05-23.title: Clinton Decision in Keeping With Some Expert Views
1999-05-23.abstract: Clinton Administration's decision to press for quick deployment of 50,000 NATO troops on Kosovo's border is endorsed by military experts and experts on Balkans; they say ground troops are essential to resolving conflict, but caution that troops should not be deployed unless political leaders are prepared to use them if a settlement does not materialize or if one collapses (M)

1999-05-24.title: Americans Are Building a Camp But Few Kosovars Are Drawn to It
1999-05-24.abstract: Americans are building refugee camp in Fier, Albania, hoping that thousands of Kosovo refugees will come there as haven until they can return to their homeland; so far, few have come, fearing to come that far south of Kosovo border; instead, they remain in Kukes, northern Albanian city, which was supposed to be temporary transit point for them; more than 100,000 refugees have put down roots in Kukes and remain there in increasingly desperate conditions; photo (M)

1999-05-24.title: American Volunteer Finds Frustrations and Joy at Crowded Refugee Clinic
1999-05-24.abstract: Dr Jennifer Walser, who left United States to volunteer in refugee camps in Macedonia month ago, has extended her stay for two weeks; says she is learning joys and frustrations of aid work, and has found that easing pain and suffering can be intoxicating; she is also learning limits of her work, and her inability to end cause of misery; says she wants to find way to make medical work for refugees regular part of her life; photo (M)

1999-05-24.title: Serbs' Dark Humor Finds Many Targets
1999-05-24.abstract: Residents of Belgrade, convinced that they are right to be fighting for Kosovo but weary of war, are telling jokes that capture their general mood of helplessness, irony and pessimism (S)

1999-05-24.title: Aznalcazar Journal; Year-Old Spill Poisons Farms and Wild Food Chain
1999-05-24.abstract: Farmland in Aznalcazar, Spain, is covered with poisonous black mud, year after waste reservoir of nearby zinc mine burst open and more than billion gallons of toxic slurry poured through breach into Guadiamar River, flooding hundreds of farms; cleanup crews have hauled away 12 million tons of noxious mud, along with topsoil and dead fish and fowl; 20-mile-long flood area now looks fairly clean, but toxic chain has spread through nature that is less visible and may be difficult to break down; high levels of heavy metals are still embedded in soil and water and have found their way into wildlife; photo; maps (M)

1999-05-24.title: Kremlinology 101: The More Things Change . . .
1999-05-24.abstract: Russia's fledgling democracy is as secretive and arcane as Communist regime it replaced seven years ago; Pres Boris Yeltsin has replaced his popular Prime Minister, Yevgeny M Primakov, in name of stopping purported Communist comeback; Primakov's coalition Cabinet included Communist ministers and was backed by Yeltsin's Communist opposition, marking first attempt by Yeltsin to rule not by confrontation, but by consensus; Russia's new Prime Minister is Sergei V Stepashin; his credentials for his new role are obscure, but his loyalty to Yeltsin is absolute; photo (M)

1999-05-25.title: China Shifts on How to Resettle Million People for Giant Dam
1999-05-25.abstract: China's Prime Min Zhu Rongji, in implicit admission that severe problems bedevil giant Three Gorges Dam project, announces major change in strategy for resettling at least 1.3 million people who will be driven from homes; says more of people who will be uprooted by new 400-mile-long lake on Yangtze River, should be sent to distant parts of country; current effort to move displaced people to sites near old homes has been plauged by public bitterness, inadequate funds and acute shortage of cropland; critics say new solution may be no more practical; photo (M)

1999-05-25.title: Protests Are Resumed by Families of Yugoslav Reservists Ordered Back to Duty in Kosovo
1999-05-25.abstract: Protests resume in towns and villages in Serbia as army reservists, supported by family members, resist orders to return to Kosovo after short leave; demonstrations reported in towns of Krusevac and Aleksandrovac ever since reservists received orders to report for duty in Kosovo after only three days' leave; follow days of protests by women of region south of Belgrade near border with Kosovo (M)

1999-05-25.title: Albright Says Hussein's Foes Are Building Unified Front
1999-05-25.abstract: US Sec of State Madeleine K Albright meets with leaders of Iraqi opposition groups; first such encounter is seen as indication of Clinton Administration's efforts to build more cohesive opposition against Pres Saddam Hussein; meeting occurs as State Dept announces moves to supply opposition with 'nonlethal' aid, such as offices and commmunications equipment; under 1998 Iraqi Liberation Act, $97 million was made available for lethal and nonlethal aid to seven groups intent on overthrowing Hussein (M)

1999-05-25.title: WHO Defers Destroying Stocks of Smallpox
1999-05-25.abstract: GENEVA—  Bowing to concerns that smallpox virus stocks be left intact to counter possible bioterrorist attacks, the World Health Organization agreed Monday to recommend delaying destruction of the world's last known virus samples until no later than the end of 2002.

1999-05-25.title: . . . While Some Americans Are Saying, 'Just Shoot the Man'
1999-05-25.abstract: Group of White Plains, NY, residents interviewed during at start, and during course, of NATO's bombing campaign against Serbia have not changed initial views, but almost all express fatigue with conflict that has lasted longer than any imagined; those who support bombing campaign says US and NATO allies cannot walk away from it now; photos (M)

1999-05-26.title: NATO MOVES AHEAD ON A KOSOVO FORCE OF 50,000 TROOPS
1999-05-26.abstract: NATO approves plans for heavily armed peacekeeping force of about 50,000 soldiers for Kosovo, part of broad drive by NATO's top commander to increase political and military pressure on Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; plan, which comes as Gen Wesley K Clark begins pushing to broaden bombing strikes, calls for troops to enter Kosovo only after Serbian troops are withdrawn; would station about 30,000 troops, including soldiers from United States, Britain, France and Germany in Macedonia, positioning allies to move swiftly into Kosovo if settlement is reached; some officials say forces could also play combat role if NATO later decides on land attack (M)

1999-05-26.title: Fear Still Accompanies Many South Africans to Polls
1999-05-26.abstract: Election-linked violence and intimidation continue in South Africa, but tensions are much lower than before 1994 vote; ruling African National Congress representatives use military escort to take their campaign to Nongoma, stronghold of rival Inkatha Freedom Party, and flee hastily in face of angry mob; map; photo (M)

1999-05-26.title: China Labels Spying Claims 'Groundless'
1999-05-26.abstract: China scores as 'groundless' charges that Chinese spies stole nuclear weapon designs and missile technology from United States; sees effort to divert attention from NATO's bombing campaign in Yugoslavia and attack on China's Belgrade Embassy (M)

1999-05-26.title: 'Leaner, Meaner' OECD Rides the Global Waves
1999-05-26.abstract: PARIS—  Since its birth in 1961, the OECD has often been portrayed as the rich man's club of multinational organizations. It is an elite economic research outfit, critics say, that has idled away its years in Paris representing the interests of the world's cosseted economies, as it assesses the economic pulse of the globe, passing policy judgments and occasionally admitting new members to its own clique.

1999-05-26.title: A Refugee River, Dammed at the Border
1999-05-26.abstract: Over 4,500 ethnic Albanians flee into Macedonia, including hundreds of women from Kacanik, Kosovo, many of whom say Serb forces separated fighting-age men from their group before expelling them; busload of refugees from Pristina say Serbian paramilitaries continue to terrorize and expel ethnic Albanians; nearly 25,000 people, most of them from Pristina, have poured into Macedonia in last five days; latest arrivals say there are thousands of others just behind them; map; photo (M)

1999-05-27.title: TRIBUNAL IS SAID TO CITE MILOSEVIC FOR WAR CRIMES
1999-05-27.abstract: Western officials say international tribunal in The Hague has decided to indict Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic for war crimes and will formally announce action on May 27; say Louise Arbour, chief prosecutor of tribunal, began investigation into responsibility for war crimes in Kosovo last year, and has intensified her inquiry in recent weeks; indictment of Milosevic, whose almost 12 years in power have coincided with wave after wave of violence in Balkans, is certain to pose added difficulties for diplomatic quest to end war in Kosovo; Milosevic remains sole interlocutor in Belgrade for Russian-led efforts that have been broadly supported by US and its allies to end war; his photo (M)

1999-05-27.title: If Lawmakers Call for Blood, They May End Up With Reno's
1999-05-27.abstract: Congressional committee's conclusion that China systematically stole nuclear secrets has roused angry lawmakers in both parties to look for high-profile official to blame, and increasingly they are taking aim at Atty Gen Janet Reno for failing to respond aggressively to threat; other officials, like national security adviser Samuel R Berger, have also come under attack; some senior Justice Dept officials blame Reno for mishandling FBI investigation of Wen Ho Lee, Los Alamos scientist under suspicion of passing secrets to Beijing, and for failing to set clear priorities in espionage cases (M)

1999-05-27.title: Air Force Is Told to Suspend Retirements
1999-05-27.abstract: Pentagon orders suspension of retirements from Air Force to insure that enough pilots and other personnel are available to continue waging NATO's air war in Balkans; order, another sign of strain war is putting on US military, immediately affects over 6,000 officers and enlisted personnel who already have indicated plans to retire between now and Dec; it will also postpone any new retirements by about 120,000 Air Force personnel with particular job skills; order affects 40 percent of Air Force's 363,000 personnel (M)

1999-05-27.title: Diplomats Trying to Find Solution Will Try Again
1999-05-27.abstract: Negotiations in Moscow on Kosovo crisis break up with no clear evidence of progress, but representatives of Russia, US and European Union plan to try again on May 27; Russians say their special envoy, Viktor S Chernomyrdin, is prepared to fly to Belgrade, suggesting that agreement is at hand on peace proposal to present to Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic (M)

1999-05-27.title: Ramon Rubial, 92, a Spaniard Who Embodied Socialist Cause
1999-05-27.abstract: Ramon Rubial, Spanish Socialist imprisoned under Franco regime, dies at age 92; photo (M)

1999-05-28.title: Energy Aide in Spying Case to Be Honored
1999-05-28.abstract: Notra Trulock, Energy Dept intelligence official who first raised alarm about China's theft of American nuclear secrets, will receive special award from Energy Sec Bill Richardson for persisting in his inquiry despite numerous bureaucratic roadblocks; Trulock will be given $10,000 and department's Special Act Award; Richardson lauds Trulock; photo (M)
